title: How to Work with Psychedelic Insights About Death and Mortality
body:
How to Work with Psychedelic Insights About Death and Mortality

Death. Mortality. Two words that can make your chest tighten or your mind race. If you’ve ever taken a psychedelic journey, you might have come face to face with these concepts in a way that’s raw, unmistakable, and maybe even a little unsettling. Psychedelics don’t sugarcoat death. They often bring it right to your doorstep, sometimes with a gentle knock, sometimes with a crash. So how do you work with those insights when you come back to everyday life? How do you carry that weight without getting crushed, or worse, pushing it away? Let’s talk about that.

First, Let’s Get Real About What’s Happening

When you’re tripping, the veil between life and death often feels thinner. You might see your own death, or the death of someone you love. You might feel the impermanence of everything around you so sharply it’s like your heart is a drumbeat counting down. It can be terrifying. Or peaceful. Or both at the same time. You’re not imagining things. Psychedelics like psilocybin, LSD, or DMT have been shown to create experiences that mimic near-death states, helping people confront mortality head on.

Look, I’m not saying every trip will do this. But if yours did, it’s a sign you’re in a place to learn something huge. If you’re here, you want to make sense of that. Right?!

Here’s the thing, though: the experience itself isn’t the lesson. It’s what you do with it after that counts. You can’t just sit with the insight and expect it to magically dissolve your fear or shift your perspective. You have to work with it. Like a muscle. Like a wound that needs care.

Step One: Ground Yourself in the Physical World

After a session that brought you face to face with death, you might feel untethered. That’s normal. Your mind has been on a wild ride. But here’s where many people get stuck: they stay in their head, trying to intellectualize what they saw or felt. Don’t do that. Instead, bring yourself back to your body.

Simple, concrete actions work best. Try this:

  • Wash your face with cold water.
  • Take slow, deep breaths - inhale for four seconds, hold for four, exhale for six.
  • Walk barefoot on grass or soil for five minutes.

These grounding moves help your nervous system settle. Truth is, your body remembers what your mind sometimes wants to forget. If you need a little extra help, diffuse some lavender or eucalyptus in your space. I like an aromatherapy essential oil diffuser (paid link) for moments like this. The scent can anchor you gently, without overwhelming.

Step Two: Name What You Felt

After a big psychedelic session, your mind might feel like a jumble of images, feelings, and thoughts. You need to sort through that mess. Write it down. Say it out loud. Talk to a trusted friend or integration coach if you have one.

Here’s a concrete example. One of my students, Sarah, had a mushroom trip where she saw herself dying in vivid detail. When we talked, she said, “It felt like my whole life was flashing in front of me - like a movie I couldn’t pause or rewind.” Sound familiar? She was overwhelmed, but naming that moment helped her feel seen. She wrote in her journal every morning for two weeks, starting with: “Yesterday, death showed up like…” This simple ritual made the abstract concrete.

If journaling feels intimidating, try something guided like a therapy journal with guided prompts (paid link). It gives you a structure so you don’t get lost in your own head.

Step Three: Sit With the Discomfort

Honestly, there’s no shortcut here. Psychedelic insights about death are often uncomfortable. You might feel sadness, fear, anger, or even guilt. That’s normal. Don’t run from it. Don’t distract yourself with Netflix or social media. Sit with it. Feel it fully.

That doesn’t mean wallowing. It means acknowledging what’s there without judgment. Imagine you’re holding a fragile bird with cracked wings. You don’t shove it away or squeeze it too tight. You hold it gently, knowing it needs time to heal.

Here’s a quick exercise: when you notice a strong feeling about mortality, pause. Take a breath. Ask yourself, “What is this feeling trying to tell me?” You might be surprised by the answer. It could be that you’re grieving a loss you didn’t realize you had. Or maybe you’re afraid of leaving things unfinished. Know what I mean?

Step Four: Learn From Others Who’ve Walked This Road

You’re not alone. Death-related insights come up for many people who work with psychedelics. Reading other people’s stories can help you feel less isolated. One book I often recommend is How to Change Your Mind by Michael Pollan (paid link). It’s a beautiful account of the history and personal experiences with psychedelics, including moments that touch on death and rebirth.

Also, check out research from organizations like MAPS. They’re doing amazing work around psychedelic-assisted therapy, including studies on terminal illness and end-of-life anxiety. Those studies show that psychedelic experiences can reduce death anxiety, but only when integrated well into your life afterward.

If you want practical tips beyond this article, I’ve written about integration strategies that can help you right here. It’s a good place to start if you’re feeling overwhelmed and need a roadmap.

Step Five: Make Peace With What You Can’t Control

Here’s the hard truth: you can’t control death. No matter how much you prepare or understand, it’s inevitable. Psychedelic experiences might show you that in a way your mind never could before. You might see that everything you hold onto is temporary, including yourself.

Does that land? It’s a lot. But here’s the gift: sometimes, seeing death clearly frees you. It helps you live better. More fully. More awake.

One client, Mark, told me after his psilocybin session, “I realized I’ve been living like I’m immortal. Now I want to spend my time on what truly matters.” That kind of clarity can come from facing mortality head on.

Step Six: Create Rituals That Honor Life and Death

Rituals help us mark transitions. They give form to feelings that are otherwise hard to express. After a psychedelic trip that revealed your mortality, consider creating your own ritual.

This could be as simple as lighting a candle every evening and sitting quietly for five minutes. Or planting a tree in memory of someone. Or writing a letter to your future self about how you want to live knowing death is near.

If you want to enhance your space for reflection, something like The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) is a great companion read. It’s not about psychedelics directly, but it dives into how trauma and deep emotional experiences are stored in the body, which can be crucial when processing big existential insights.

Step Seven: Keep Coming Back to the Question, “What Does This Mean For How I Live?”

Insights about death aren’t just metaphysical puzzles. They’re invitations to change. To live differently. To care deeper. To let go of petty grudges and wasted time.

Ask yourself regularly: How do I want to show up today, knowing everything is temporary? How do I want to love differently? How do I want to work, rest, play, and connect?

Remember, this isn’t a one-time question. It’s a practice. Like meditation or yoga, it needs to be revisited. Sometimes you’ll feel inspired, other times scared. Both are okay.

Final Thoughts

Working with psychedelic insights about death and mortality isn’t easy. It’s messy, raw, and sometimes downright scary. But it’s also a chance to live more honestly and fully. You don’t have to go it alone. Reach out, read widely, write often, and treat your body like the sacred home it is.

If you want to explore more about conscious healing and psychedelic wellness, come visit me at kalesh.love. We’ll take it step by step, with kindness and clarity.

Disclaimer: This content is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult a qualified healthcare provider before making changes to your health regimen.
